html: Boards are seeing more risk data than ever. They are not necessarily seeing more clarity.
text: Boards are seeing more risk data than ever. They are not necessarily seeing more clarity.

html: Slide decks get thicker. Dashboards multiply. Metrics shift from meeting to meeting. Directors leave with more charts, but not always a better understanding of where risk-related costs are going – or what management is doing about them.
text: Slide decks get thicker. Dashboards multiply. Metrics shift from meeting to meeting. Directors leave with more charts, but not always a better understanding of where risk-related costs are going – or what management is doing about them.

html: A TCRO-driven dashboard cuts through that noise. It gives the board a small, stable set of metrics that connect risk to financial outcomes and human consequences, without dragging them into system-level detail.
text: A TCRO-driven dashboard cuts through that noise. It gives the board a small, stable set of metrics that connect risk to financial outcomes and human consequences, without dragging them into system-level detail.

html: Why board dashboards often fail
text: Why board dashboards often fail

html: Most risk dashboards fail at the board level for three reasons:
text: Most risk dashboards fail at the board level for three reasons:

html: Too many metrics. Dozens of charts, each with small importance, and no clear sense of which matter most.
text: Too many metrics. Dozens of charts, each with small importance, and no clear sense of which matter most.

html: Too much technical detail. Temperature curves, sensor counts, and alarm volumes without a clear financial frame.
text: Too much technical detail. Temperature curves, sensor counts, and alarm volumes without a clear financial frame.

html: Too little consistency. Metrics and definitions change, forcing directors to relearn the story every quarter.
text: Too little consistency. Metrics and definitions change, forcing directors to relearn the story every quarter.

html: The result is “risk fatigue.” Directors become skeptical of the numbers and default to simple questions: “Are we okay?” and “What keeps you up at night?”
text: The result is “risk fatigue.” Directors become skeptical of the numbers and default to simple questions: “Are we okay?” and “What keeps you up at night?”

html: A TCRO dashboard is designed to answer those questions consistently. It ties directly to Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk.
text: A TCRO dashboard is designed to answer those questions consistently. It ties directly to Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk [link to /blog/total-cost-of-risk-ownership].

html: What are the five TCRO metrics the board actually needs?
text: What are the five TCRO metrics the board actually needs?

html: At board level, you can usually cover the risk picture with five metrics:
text: At board level, you can usually cover the risk picture with five metrics:

html: Headline TCRO level.
text: Headline TCRO level.

html: Total Cost of Risk Ownership for the portfolio over the last 12 months.
text: Total Cost of Risk Ownership for the portfolio over the last 12 months.

html: Shown as a number and as a percentage of revenue or NOI.
text: Shown as a number and as a percentage of revenue or NOI.

html: TCRO trend.
text: TCRO trend.

html: How TCRO has moved over the last 3–5 years.
text: How TCRO has moved over the last 3–5 years.

html: Direction and magnitude matter more than exact precision.
text: Direction and magnitude matter more than exact precision.

html: Top TCRO drivers.
text: Top TCRO drivers.

html: The three or four components contributing most: losses, downtime, premiums, operating inefficiency, governance overhead.
text: The three or four components contributing most: losses, downtime, premiums, operating inefficiency, governance overhead.

html: Shown by segment (for example, seniors vs office vs mixed-use).
text: Shown by segment (for example, seniors vs office vs mixed-use).

html: Avoided losses and events.
text: Avoided losses and events.

html: Claims and incidents that did not happen because of predictive operations, improved maintenance, or capital projects.
text: Claims and incidents that did not happen because of predictive operations, improved maintenance, or capital projects.

html: Expressed in financial terms and, where appropriate, human terms.
text: Expressed in financial terms and, where appropriate, human terms.

html: Capital and insurance alignment.
text: Capital and insurance alignment.

html: How capital projects and program design are expected to change TCRO over the next 1–3 years.
text: How capital projects and program design are expected to change TCRO over the next 1–3 years.

html: The link between today’s spend and tomorrow’s risk.
text: The link between today’s spend and tomorrow’s risk.

html: Everything else – sensor counts, algorithm accuracy, alert volumes – belongs in management-level dashboards, not in the board pack.
text: Everything else – sensor counts, algorithm accuracy, alert volumes – belongs in management-level dashboards, not in the board pack.

html: How should a TCRO dashboard look and feel?
text: How should a TCRO dashboard look and feel?

html: The dashboard should be readable in minutes, not hours. That usually means:
text: The dashboard should be readable in minutes, not hours. That usually means:

html: One primary slide or screen with the five metrics above.
text: One primary slide or screen with the five metrics above.

html: Simple visuals: one or two line charts, a short ranked list, and a small table or graphic for avoided losses.
text: Simple visuals: one or two line charts, a short ranked list, and a small table or graphic for avoided losses.

html: Clear annotations explaining what changed and why.
text: Clear annotations explaining what changed and why.

html: A typical layout:
text: A typical layout:

html: Top left: headline TCRO and prior-year comparison.
text: Top left: headline TCRO and prior-year comparison.

html: Top right: 3–5 year TCRO trend.
text: Top right: 3–5 year TCRO trend.

html: Bottom left: top drivers and segments.
text: Bottom left: top drivers and segments.

html: Bottom right: avoided losses, with a call-out for the most significant event and its human impact.
text: Bottom right: avoided losses, with a call-out for the most significant event and its human impact.

html: If directors want more, they can click or ask. But the core story is always available at a glance.
text: If directors want more, they can click or ask. But the core story is always available at a glance.

html: For ideas on which drivers to highlight by asset class, see TCRO in the Real World: Seniors, Office, and Mixed-Use Portfolios.
text: For ideas on which drivers to highlight by asset class, see TCRO in the Real World: Seniors, Office, and Mixed-Use Portfolios [link to /blog/tcro-real-world-asset-classes].

html: How does predictive operations show up on the dashboard?
text: How does predictive operations show up on the dashboard?

html: Boards do not need to see raw telemetry. They do need to see how predictive operations change TCRO.
text: Boards do not need to see raw telemetry. They do need to see how predictive operations change TCRO.

html: On the dashboard, that can show up as:
text: On the dashboard, that can show up as:

html: A segment of avoided losses labeled “Predictive operations impact,” with one or two concrete examples.
text: A segment of avoided losses labeled “Predictive operations impact,” with one or two concrete examples.

html: A note on trend charts showing where predictive maintenance or monitoring was rolled out and how TCRO shifted afterward.
text: A note on trend charts showing where predictive maintenance or monitoring was rolled out and how TCRO shifted afterward.

html: A brief link between predictive operations, capital projects, and insurance outcomes.
text: A brief link between predictive operations, capital projects, and insurance outcomes.

html: For the underlying operating-model detail, directors can be pointed to From Reactive to Predictive: Why Your Maintenance Model Is Now a Finance Strategy.
text: For the underlying operating-model detail, directors can be pointed to From Reactive to Predictive: Why Your Maintenance Model Is Now a Finance Strategy [link to /blog/reactive-to-predictive-maintenance-real-estate].

html: How does data governance support a credible TCRO dashboard?
text: How does data governance support a credible TCRO dashboard?

html: A TCRO dashboard only works if directors trust the numbers.
text: A TCRO dashboard only works if directors trust the numbers.

html: That trust comes from:
text: That trust comes from:

html: Clear ownership of the data behind each metric.
text: Clear ownership of the data behind each metric.

html: Stable definitions over time.
text: Stable definitions over time.

html: The ability to trace a board-level KPI back to underlying systems and events when needed.
text: The ability to trace a board-level KPI back to underlying systems and events when needed.

html: In other words, it comes from governance. Without Data Governance in place, a TCRO dashboard is just another set of numbers that might change next quarter.
text: In other words, it comes from governance. Without Data Governance in place, a TCRO dashboard is just another set of numbers that might change next quarter [link to /blog/data-governance-real-estate].

html: The board does not need to see the governance framework. They need to hear, simply and confidently, that:
text: The board does not need to see the governance framework. They need to hear, simply and confidently, that:

html: “These metrics come from a single source of truth.”
text: “These metrics come from a single source of truth.”

html: “Definitions are controlled and stable.”
text: “Definitions are controlled and stable.”

html: “We can show you the underlying evidence for any point on this chart.”
text: “We can show you the underlying evidence for any point on this chart.”

html: How should management use the dashboard in the boardroom?
text: How should management use the dashboard in the boardroom?

html: The TCRO dashboard is not just a reporting tool. It is a conversation tool.
text: The TCRO dashboard is not just a reporting tool. It is a conversation tool.

html: In practice, management should use it to:
text: In practice, management should use it to:

html: Open the risk section with a concise story: where TCRO is, where it is going, what is driving it.
text: Open the risk section with a concise story: where TCRO is, where it is going, what is driving it.

html: Frame capital proposals: which TCRO drivers a project addresses and what impact is expected.
text: Frame capital proposals: which TCRO drivers a project addresses and what impact is expected.

html: Frame insurance discussions: how continuous data and program design are expected to change premiums and retained risk.
text: Frame insurance discussions: how continuous data and program design are expected to change premiums and retained risk.

html: The goal is that directors can answer, in their own words:
text: The goal is that directors can answer, in their own words:

html: “What is our Total Cost of Risk Ownership?”
text: “What is our Total Cost of Risk Ownership?”

html: “Is it moving in the right direction?”
text: “Is it moving in the right direction?”

html: “Do we have a credible plan to improve it – and is that plan being executed?”
text: “Do we have a credible plan to improve it – and is that plan being executed?”

html: For capital-specific detail, they can refer to How TCRO Changes Capital Planning: From Age-Based Replacement to Risk-Weighted Investment. For insurance, they can read Insurance Is No Longer a Fixed Line Item.
text: For capital-specific detail, they can refer to How TCRO Changes Capital Planning: From Age-Based Replacement to Risk-Weighted Investment [link to /blog/tcro-capital-planning]. For insurance, they can read Insurance Is No Longer a Fixed Line Item [link to /blog/data-driven-insurance-renewals-real-estate].

html: Where to go from here
text: Where to go from here

html: If you want to build or refine your own TCRO dashboard:
text: If you want to build or refine your own TCRO dashboard:

html: Start with a simple TCRO model. Use the TCRO calculator to get a first view of TCRO level and drivers with your own assumptions. Use that as a draft for what the board-level metrics could look like.
text: Start with a simple TCRO model. Use the TCRO calculator to get a first view of TCRO level and drivers with your own assumptions [link to /tcro#calculator]. Use that as a draft for what the board-level metrics could look like.

html: Walk through your current board pack with an expert. Use Talk to an Expert to review how you currently report risk and capital to the board and identify where a TCRO dashboard could replace or simplify existing material.
text: Walk through your current board pack with an expert. Use Talk to an Expert to review how you currently report risk and capital to the board and identify where a TCRO dashboard could replace or simplify existing material [link to your contact modal or /contact].

html: When TCRO is presented this way, boards get what they need most: a clear sense of risk-related costs, confidence that management is in control, and a direct line of sight between today’s decisions and tomorrow’s outcomes for both the balance sheet and the people inside your buildings.
text: When TCRO is presented this way, boards get what they need most: a clear sense of risk-related costs, confidence that management is in control, and a direct line of sight between today’s decisions and tomorrow’s outcomes for both the balance sheet and the people inside your buildings.
